title: Pad Kee Mao

overview.description: A fiery Thai stir-fry of wide rice noodles, chicken, vegetables and fragrant basil in a savoury sauce. Quick to cook, full of bold flavours and best prepared over very high heat.

ingredients: 200g fresh wide rice noodles (or dried, prepared according to the packet)
150g green beans, cut into bite-sized pieces
method: Mix together the oyster sauce, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, fish sauce and sugar.
Heat a wok over a very high heat until just beginning to smoke.
Add the vegetable oil followed immediately by the chicken. Stir-fry for about 3–4 minutes until almost cooked through.
Add the garlic and chillies and stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the onion, pepper and green beans and stir-fry for about 2 minutes. The vegetables should still have some bite.
Add the noodles and pour over the prepared sauce.
Toss everything together over the high heat until the noodles are evenly coated and beginning to caramelise in places.
Turn off the heat and immediately stir through the Thai basil until just wilted.

learning.did-you-know: Despite the name meaning 'drunken noodles', Pad Kee Mao traditionally contains no alcohol. The name is thought to refer either to its fiery heat or its popularity after a night out.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare ingredients before cooking, why high heat is essential for stir-frying, and how basil transforms a dish when added at the very end.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the quickest meals in the planner and a fantastic introduction to authentic Thai stir-frying. It delivers huge flavour with relatively few ingredients.
chapman-tips: Have every ingredient prepared before turning on the heat—the cooking only takes a few minutes.
Thai basil is easier to find in the UK and makes an excellent substitute for traditional holy basil.
Don't overcrowd the wok. Cook in batches if necessary.
A carbon steel wok produces the best results.
notes.batch-cooking: Not recommended. Best cooked and eaten immediately.
notes.leftovers: Reheat quickly in a hot wok or frying pan. The noodles will soften slightly but will still taste excellent.
